```
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by failsafeDexconf, using

		Depth	24
		Virtual	1280	960

	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen 0 "Default Screen" 0 0
EndSection
Section "Module"
	Load		"glx"
	Load		"GLcore"
	Load		"v4l"
EndSection
Section "device" # 
	Identifier	"device1"
	Boardname	"vesa"
	Busid		"PCI:3:0:0"
	Driver		"vesa"
	Screen	1
EndSection
Section "screen" #

	Defaultdepth	24
	Monitor		"monitor1"
EndSection
Section "monitor" #

EndSection
Section "ServerFlags"
EndSection
```

I've tried using EnvyNG, but this is yielding no results. I've tried using the official nvidia driver but that's not giving me anything either.

Does anyone have any clue what this is? There's a few people reporting it, but they seemed to have managed to work around it, yet whenever i've tried using their methods they don't seem to have worked for me.

I've got two monitors plugged in, i don't know if that's the trouble. at the moment the display is cloned. I've tried using both as primary and disabling the others but still nothing.

Any help would be amazing!!

### Post by Mazin on 2008-06-25
From the xorg.conf you posted, apparently Envy didn't in fact install them fine.  Otherwise, under Section "Device" > Driver, it would say "nvidia" instead of "vesa".  "Vesa" is the fallback driver for when nothing else works; Envy should have changed this to "nvidia".

Try this: it's a bit risky, but as long as you backup your current xorg.conf you should be fine.

In 'Section "Device"' (both of them, I would guess, or change one and comment out the second device section)
Comment out the "boardname" line
change the driver option from "vesa" to "nv"

"nv" is the open-source nvidia driver, which should give you full resolution but not 3d acceleration

Restart X (Ctrl+Alt+Backspace)

If that works, go and change "nv" to "nvidia", and restart.

It looks like your xorg.conf is a bit abnormal.  I'm not positive, but Envy should've said something to you or gave you an option to change your xorg.conf; perhaps you didn't pick it or something.  There's also a command to go through the wizard to reconfigure your X.org, "sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg" that might give you a fresh start.  I'd focus on getting one monitor to work first.  And, of course, in case you do totally break your configuration, you always have a text terminal handy---just push Ctrl+Alt+F1 (or F2 through F6).  That's a lot of instructions and tips, but these things are just really hard to diagnose without my having access to the computer.  It's like playing long-distance doctor :)

---

### Post by l0rz on 2008-06-26
Here's my latest xorg.conf after doing a clean install of ubuntu, using EnvyNG and viewing the file.

I don't know if anyone will be able to see something in this now?

Thanks

```
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
	Option		"AddARGBGLXVisuals"	"True"
	Defaultdepth	24
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
	Driver		"nvidia"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"gb"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen "Default Screen"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option		"Composite"	"Enable"
EndSection

```

### Post by Mazin on 2008-06-26
nvidia-settings is the official tool to change your video settings for nvidia cards.  It should let you do whatever your card can.  Also, are you seeing the nvidia logo flash on your screen when you start Ubuntu?  That's an indicator that it's actually using the nvidia driver.

### Post by earthmeLon on 2008-06-27
Here is my xorg.conf

```
 
# nvidia-settings: X configuration file generated by nvidia-settings
# nvidia-settings:  version 1.0  (buildd@yellow)  Tue Mar  4 20:28:57 UTC 2008
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Option		"AddARGBGLXVisuals"	"True"
	Defaultdepth	24
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Screen0"
	Device		"Videocard0"
	Monitor		"Monitor0"
	Option		"TwinView"	"1"
	Option		"TwinViewXineramaInfoOrder"	"DFP-1"
	Option		"metamodes"	"DFP-0: nvidia-auto-select +1440+0, DFP-1: nvidia-auto-select +0+0"
	Option		"AddARGBGLXVisuals"	"True"
	Defaultdepth	24
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	Option		"NoLogo"	"True"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Videocard0"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	Vendorname	"NVIDIA Corporation"
	Boardname	"GeForce 8800 GTX"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen 0 "Screen0" 0 0
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load		"glx"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Monitor0"
	Vendorname	"Unknown"
	Modelname	"DELL E228WFP"
	Horizsync	30.0	-	83.0
	Vertrefresh	56.0	-	75.0
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
	Option		"Xinerama"	"0"
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option		"Composite"	"Enable"
EndSection


```

I have two monitors (22" LG and 17" DELL)  The dell one just died v_v, so now only one is actually working.  Maybe looking over this config file might help you to manipulate yours.

To test your new configuration, instead of having to reboot, I think you can just press ctrl+alt+bksp to restart  xserver :D

### Post by Mazin on 2008-06-28
Try running, in a terminal,

xrandr -s 1600x1200

and see if that works or not.

Edit: In case that does work, take a look at [http://fluxbox-wiki.org/index.php/Howto_change_resolution](http://fluxbox-wiki.org/index.php/Howto_change_resolution)
